California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January L4 | L99l
Honorable Diane E. watson
Senator, 28th District
California State Senate
State capitol, Roon 4o4o
Sacramento, cA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Informa] Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-9 0-7 4 2
Dear Senator watson:
You hav6 requested advice under tlle new ethics provisions of
the Po1itical Reforn Act (the na6grr1.1/ Because your request is a
general inquiry, we are treating your request as one- for -informal
iriiit.r,"" 
-purluant to RegnrlatiSn- 18329 (6) (copy enclosed) .2/
OUESTIONS
1. If a Member of the Legislature joins a speakers' bureau
as an independent contractor, may he or she be paid for speaking
2. If not, what, if any, condition or restriction may make
such an arrangement possible?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Section 89502 of the Ethics in Government Act of 1990
prohibits elected state officers from accepting an honorarium for
any speech gj.ven, regardLess of the content of the speech. The
stitute creates an exception to the honorarium ban for income
L/ Government code sections 81ooo-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
commission regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations
Sectj-on 18ooo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ fnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suire 8OO . P.O. Box 8o7 . Sacramento CA 958o4-o8o7 . (916)322.5660


Our Fi■e No. I-90-742
Page 2
earned for a speech given by a state officer in connection with
the practice of a bona fide business, trade or profession.
However, this exception does not apply l^rhen the sole or
predorninant activity of the Member's busi,ness, trade or profession
is making speeches. Incone earned from the practice of naking
speeches, such as a speakers' panel, is deemed to be an
honorariurn. Thus, if a Menber of the Legislature joins a
speakers' bureau, he or she is prohibited from acceptinq oavment
for a speech given.
2. A Menber of the LegisLature is not precluded from joining
a speakers' bureau and receiving pa)anent for rnaking speeches in
connection therewith if the Member does not use the honorariun,
and within 30 days the Member either returns the honorariurn to the
donor or donates the honorarium to the State's Generaf Fund
without takincr a deduction for tax purDoses.
Sect i on
provides, irl.
ANALYSIS
89502 of the Ethics in Covernment Act Of 
■990
pertinent part′  as fo■■oWS:
No e■ected state officer may accept an
(a)
honorariurn.
(b) rrHonorariumrr means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any paynent nade in consideration
for any speech given, articl-e published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, soci,al- event, meal, or like
gathering.
(c) rrHonorariunrr does not include earned
income for personal services which are custornarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona
fide business, trade, or profess.ion, such as
teaching, practicing 1aw, nedicine, insurance, real
estate, banking, or buitding contracting, unless
the sole or predorninant aetivitv of the business.
trade. or profession is rnakinq speeches. The
commission shall adopt regulations to iurplement
this subdivision.
(d) Section 89506 shall apply to all
paynents, advances, and reimbursements for travel
and rel-ated lodging and subsistence.
(e) This section does not apply to any
honorarium which is not used and, within 30 days
after receipt, is either returned to the donor or


delivered to the control-ler for donation to the
ceneral Fund, without being claimed as a deduction
from income tax purposes.
secti,on 89502 (a) -(e) , (enPhasis
added).
The former statute relating to the limitations on gifts and
honorarj.um, secti.on 85400, had placed a S1000 lirnit on any speech
relating to the governmental process. Jllis statute was repealed
and repiaced by sections 8950i - 89506'3/ under the current
statutL, state el-ected of f i.cers are prohibited from accepting an
honorarium for any speech given, regardless of the content of the
speech .
The statute creates an exception to the honorarium ban for
income earned for a speech given by a state officer in connection
with the practice of I bona fide business, trade, or profession'
However, this exception does not apply $rhen the sole or
predorninant activity of the Member's business, trade or profession
is naking speeches. Incone earned frorn the practice of making
speeches; such as a speakers' panel, is deerned to be honorarium.
thus, in answer to your first question, if a Member of the
Legisl-ature joins a speakers' bureau, he or she is prohibited fron
acceptj-nq pavment for a speech given.
You aLso asked whether there lras any condition or restriction
srhich e/ouLd make joining a speakers' bureau Permissible. Clearly,
a Member of the Legislaiure is not precluded from joining a
speakers' bureau and receiving payment for rnaking speeches in
connection therewith under the fotlowing conditi-ons:
( 1 ) The I'Ienber does not use the honorarium;
(2) within 30 days the ltember either returns the honorarium
to the donor or donates the honorariurn to the State's General Fund
in accordance $/ith subdivision (e) above.
The provj.so is that the Member does not clairn the honorarium as a
deduction fro:n income for tax purposes. In this event, the
honorariuro has no value to the state officer and need not be
d i sclosed .

as an independent contractor, may he or she be paid for speaking
2. If not, what, if any, condition or restriction may make
such an arrangement possible?

CONCLUSION: 1. Section 89502 of the Ethics in Government Act of 1990
prohibits elected state officers from accepting an honorarium for
any speech gj.ven, regardLess of the content of the speech. The
stitute creates an exception to the honorarium ban for income
